title: General Aiptasia husbandry - Pringle Lab
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2pma9","text":"This protocol describes the basic steps neccessary to maintain and propagate Aiptasia as they are done in the Pringle lab. It is a fork to the original protocol with more detailed information and updated procedures.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"7l0s5","text":"If working with symbiotic anemones hosting different symbiodiniacaean algal strains make sure to keep all working surfaces clean and wash/sanitize your hands regularly.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9t8ur","text":"Keep different strain combinations as separated as possible (e.g. different shelves or incubator compartments). Do water changes and tank cleaning for one host-symbiont combination at a time. Sanitize hands and surfaces after finishing one batch of host-symbiont combination.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c7dh9","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cu2pb","text":"Aiptasia is a very robust organism. Especially the symbiotic animals are more sensitive to impaired water quality then to short-term starvation (1-2 weeks).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"415le","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fufmc","text":"The bigger the anemones the more feeding they need to keep their body mass.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"2qq77","text":"It is absolutely critical to use a bleach without added detergents, surfactants and chelating agents!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":101}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8vhkj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ebst2","text":"The professional-grade ChloroxPro germicidal bleach has a higher hypochlorite content (8.25% vs. 5-6% for most household bleach products).  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":18},{"style":"bold","offset":30,"length":3},{"style":"bold","offset":139,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3lar8","text":"The retail bleach products from Chlorox all contain polydiallyldimethylammounium chloride, polyacrylic acid and/or other additives which interfere with the protocol (they most likely masking or changing the negative charge of the nucleic acids, in the worst case, the gels are blank). Only use bleach for which the full list of ingredients is available not just the \"active ingredient\", it should only contain the following:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":52}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3j6fd","text":"sodium hypochlorite ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"89ep3","text":"sodium hydroxide ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2ilm7","text":"sodium chloride","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8mt3c","text":"sodium carbonate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"691db","text":"sodium chlorate ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d3kq2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"24fv3","text":"SAFETY REMARKS: Bleach is toxic and corrosive chemical. Handle with care, wear PPE when working with it. NEVER mix bleach with anything except for (salt-) water, highly toxic and volatile chemicals such as chlorine gas can be produced. Dispose of hazardous chemicals according to your institution's chemical waste guidelines.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":105,"length":130}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"u2UYR","text":"Equipment:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7kosk","text":"Percival E41 \u0026 AL41 light- and temperature-controlled incubators","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":9,"length":3},{"key":1,"offset":15,"length":4}],"data":{}},{"key":"cshgl","text":"Apogee MQ-200 or MQ-500 PAR meter","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":7,"length":16}],"data":{}},{"key":"df827","text":"Onset HOBO loggers: ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"db2au","text":"     - LEGACY: Onset HOBO UA-002-64 (requires extra optical read-out device and HOBOware app for macOS and Windows)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":15,"length":20},{"key":4,"offset":80,"length":8}],"data":{}},{"key":"crcq3","text":"     - PREFERRED: Onset HOBO MX2202 (set-up and read-out via Bluetooth and HOBOconnect app for iOS, Android and Windows)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":5,"offset":18,"length":17},{"key":6,"offset":75,"length":11}],"data":{}},{"key":"b7msi","text":"Salinity refractometer with automatic temperature compensation, e.g., Vee-Gee STX-3","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":64,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":7,"offset":70,"length":13}],"data":{}},{"key":"d0mn","text":"Water pumps for the ASW system: Little Giant - 2-MDQX-SC (in-line, magnetic drive, 1/30 hp, 770 gal./h capacity., 1\" NPT female/male inlet/outlet). ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":8,"offset":32,"length":24}],"data":{}},{"key":"9lgdm","text":"We have one pump for mixing ASW and then transferring it to the storage barrel and one pump moving the ASW from the storage barrel through the three filters to the taps. It is recommended to always have one spare pump as the salt water is degrading them over time.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":264}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4m0jq","text":"10″×2.5″ filter housings, e.g., PureT - C891-BK34-PR","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":26,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3phnb","text":"PVC pipes, connectors and different valves and pressure gauges for the water system","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f4mcf","text":"Heavy-duty plastic 55-gal barrels (one white/translucent for mixing, one black for storage/dispensal), e.g., McMaster-Carr - 4319T58 and 4319T971","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":103,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":9,"offset":125,"length":7},{"key":10,"offset":137,"length":8}],"data":{}},{"key":"44g5e","text":"20-L brine shrimp hatching cone, e.g., Aquaneering - 3D-printed replacement feet: printables.com","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":12,"offset":36,"length":14}],"data":{}},{"key":"52thm","text":"Outdoor LED spot light, 20-30 W,  15-30° beam angle, 5000 K, 3000-4000 Lumen","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e1sdt","text":"     E.g., this one from superbrightleds.com, the one we use is discontinued.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[{"key":13,"offset":25,"length":19},{"key":14,"offset":64,"length":12}],"data":{}},{"key":"e0rt0","text":"Air pump, silent, 25 W, 3/8\" outlet, Whitewater - 100533032 (5 W)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":61,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":17,"offset":76,"length":15}],"data":{}},{"key":"90kqk","text":"Digital aquarium thermometer with submersible, plastic encased probe, e.g., Zacro - ZDT1D-AUX-1","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":18,"offset":84,"length":11}],"data":{}},{"key":"d1k7d","text":"Brine shrimp net (≤150 µm mesh openings), e.g., Aquatop Brine Shrimp Net 4\" (4×3″) ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":42,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":19,"offset":48,"length":34}],"data":{}},{"key":"ufbf","text":"Pentair Aquatic Ecosystems used to sell 4×6″ and 8×10″ brine shrimp nets but discontinued them, 4×6″ was the perfect size for the described scale of hatching. Now, the only source of larger than 4×3″ nets seems to be this: https://floridaaquafarms.com/shop/brine-shrimp-hand-nets/. Nylon filter bags for brewing/food processing could be an alternative for DIY nets.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":365}],"entityRanges":[{"key":20,"offset":223,"length":57}],"data":{}},{"key":"4o1or","text":"Sponge stick/deep-reach algae scrubber, e.g., from aquacave.com","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":40,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":21,"offset":51,"length":12}],"data":{}},{"key":"56ic0","text":"Plastic crates for bleach and DI baths, 20 gal. total capacity, used for 46 L liquid: Rubbermaid Commercial - FG9S3100WHT","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":22,"offset":110,"length":11}],"data":{}},{"key":"9sKqa","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3h54e","text":"Additional equipment for magnetic brine shrimp cysts:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":53}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6hbil","text":"12 Neodymium magnets, N52, 1″ long x 1/2″ wide x 1/2″ thick, magnetized through thickness","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8pn9o","text":"     E.g., K\u0026J Magnetics - BX088-N52","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[{"key":23,"offset":11,"length":25}],"data":{}},{"key":"d3bqh","text":"CAUTION: These are very strong magnets which need to be handled with care to prevent injuries, Neodymium magnets are also very brittle, if they smash together, shards will break off. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":183},{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"79lg0","text":"12 3D-printed enclosures for individual magnets with threaded connectors and a handle","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"57c2u","text":"~21″ long, rigid, clear PETG tube, 1-1/4\" OD × 1-1/8\" ID, e.g., McMaster-Carr - 9245K41","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":24,"offset":64,"length":23}],"data":{}},{"key":"bp1ap","text":"3D-printed bottom cover glued water tight to tube with epoxy","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fm076","text":"3D-printed top adapter assembly to attach tube to hatching-cone lid","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"94jas","text":"All 3D-printing files can be found on printables.com.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":25,"offset":38,"length":14}],"data":{}},{"key":"gqeu","text":"If no 3D-printer is available, the are several commercial online services which produce and ship 3D-prints on demand.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":117}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5mtb7","text":"Possible workarounds with no 3D-prints:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a1aln","text":"The magnets need to be assembled into a rod on the square sides, we have 3D-printed individual housings which can be screwed together to a ~15″-long rod with a magnet-free extension to ~24″.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":192}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b4l79","text":"Using a rigid plastic tube with 0.5″ ID and some plastic separators to prevent them from smashing together should also work. In the end, you want to have one, long rod of the magnets which can be inserted from the top into the wider tube attached to the lid of the hatching cone.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":279}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"doufm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d331r","text":"Consumables, plastic and glass ware:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":36}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bdb8l","text":"LevGo® smartSpatula® PolyPropylene Disposable Lab Spatulas - 17211","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"850b3","text":"Polyethylene transfer pipettes, large orifice, e.g., Thermo Scientific - 691TS or VWR - 414004-032","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":47,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":79,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9lf6","text":"6″ Tapered Mini-tip Cotton Swab, Puritan - 826-WC ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qd24","text":"     These are for delicate cleaning, especially in multi-well plates.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":70}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fjj4e","text":"1-L Imhoff cones, e.g., Wheaton - W990800","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f3p79","text":"Imhoff-cone stand. 905E6F25E43B484E9505D7B32B251","url":"https://alconox.com/product/citranox/"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D181A082F2B34388B7BD788E8D14A07E","url":"https://www.onsetcomp.com/products/software/hoboware"}},"40":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"4050066AEE0B43128FB6A57491B502DE","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/US/en/product/aldrich/z273236"}},"5":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"10DAD64866714380942D40F1CA96DBB8","url":"https://www.onsetcomp.com/products/data-loggers/mx2202"}},"6":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"1394BA090ABC474388C838FBA711074D","url":"https://www.onsetcomp.com/products/software/hoboconnect"}},"7":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"18A010C31DF54B788ED32B028F94E86B","url":"https://www.heathrowscientific.com/handheld-salinity-refractometer/"}},"8":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"983C6EB2759C4BCF82E906B9A8FCADFB","url":"https://www.littlegiant.com/products/pool-equipment-and-water-garden/aquarium-pumps/magnetic-drive/mdq-series/mdq-order-information/580507/"}},"9":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"1D958F3561B311F0B2320A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://www.mcmaster.com/4319T58"}}}}
steps: Incubators: We use temperature- and light-controlled incubators from the Percival E41 & AL41 series. The Pringle lab uses Philips F25T8/TL841 fluorescent lamps in the incubators which have a color temperature of 4100 K and a CRI of 80. The Cleves lab uses the same incubators but with SciWhite LED lighting with 4000 K and a CRI=90. For simply safe-keeping/archival of different anemone strains and backups of the routine stock strains, we keep the animals outside of incubators on shelves at room temperature (22°C-25°C) with fluorescent light fixtures. The lower temperature reduces pedal laceration, growth and biofilm formation. Light intensity can be easily adjusted using neutral density lighting gels for movie and stage productions, they are resistant to the heat of the tubes and can be simply wrapped around them as many times as needed to get the intensity to the desired level. We use Rosco e-colour+ Lighting Gel - .3 neutral density - E209

Artificial seawater (ASW): We use artificial seawater in the lab, mixed in de-ionized water from Red Sea Coral Pro salt: The salinity is adjusted to be between 32 and 34 g/L (equal to ppt - as read on a refractometer). The pH is usally slightly above 8.0. We mix our batches of seawater in a 55-gallon (208 L) white plastic barrel with a pump for mixing the water over 4-16 h. Subsequently, salinity is checked and adjusted by either adding de-ionized water or some more salt. Then, the water gets pumped into a second (storage), black 55-gallon barrel via a pipe connection. This second container is connected to pipes leading to taps for retrieving ready-to-use ASW. Between the barrel and the taps sits a filtration system (5 µm, 1 µm and 0.35 µm) to filter out particles and microorganisms. Filters are changed monthly. Advantages of using ASW over natural seawater are easy adjustment of the salinity, a relatively controlled composition (including batch-to-batch quality control and analysis by the manufacturer) and absence of potential undesired compounds such as metabolites, toxins or signaling molecules. Other aquarium salt mixes such as Instant Ocean work as well but differ in the exact composition and pH. Decide for one product and do not mix them up (especially not in between or within experiments).

Culturing containers: Polycarbonate food containers are good for maintaining low to medium numbers (≤200 small to mid-sized anemones) of different clonal lines or experimental groups. Don’t overcrowd them. We mainly use Camwear containers from Cambro; they are made from food-grade polycarbonate and are available in a large variety of sizes in clear as well as in black to maintain anemones in darkness. The sizes we use are the following (anemone numbers for mid-sized, 2-5 mm oral disc polyps): 1/9 size, 2.5″ (6.5 cm) deep: 250 mL ASW; 1-20 anemones. 1/6 size, 2.5″ (6.5 cm) deep: 500 mL ASW; 20-50 anemones. 1/4 size, 4″ (10 cm) deep: 1.5 L ASW; 50-200 anemones.

Feeding : Anemones are fed at least once per week. To maximize feeding, the container of anemones can be saturated with brine shrimp but requires more thorough cleaning. Big and/or aposymbiotic anemones need more frequent feeding - usually at least twice a week. Be careful not to allow old food to stay in the containers, as this will quickly foul the water (especially in the smaller containers). Increasing the number of days a week of feeding increases the growth rate of the anemones.

Cleaning tanks with Aiptasia: Clean tanks and replace ASW after feeding (wait at least 1 hr, but preferably >4 h, and no longer than 8 h), with additional cleaning/water changes if necessary. Water can simply be dumped, just look out for any detached anemones and try not to lose them. For anemones in the standard tanks, we use disposable polypropylene spatulas to scrape off biofilm and unwanted pedal lacerates. This is far more effective and less time-consuming than using cotton swaps. We only use fine-tipped cotton swaps for anemones kept in multi-well plates. If a tank cannot be cleaned well enough any more, dislodge the anemones and transfer them into a new tank with fresh ASW. If you see any dying/unhealthy anemones, isolate container and decide on treatment. Causes can be disease, hypoxia, salinity, temperature, bleach contamination, or accumulation of microbes on waste food. If the anemones in the container are dispensable, add 5% (v/v) bleach to the water, let sit >24 h at room temperature for everything in the tub to dissolve, there should be no big particulate matter remain just some whitish precipitate. Then clean the tub thoroughly and proceed bleaching containers (step 7 ff. in this protocol). If they they are, e.g. , an important strain with no backups, keep them isolated from all other tanks, clean as much biofilm out of the tank as possible (depending on how bad they look, a tank change might be preferable but imposes additional stress) and give the tank daily water changes for a week. If the condition improves, contrinue for one more week and place them back to the other tanks if it is clear that they'll recover. In case they don't recover, they might be infected with some detrimental microorganism. In that case it can be worth a try to use an antibiotic treatment. We have used the following antibiotics (or combinations thereof to target different bacteria and cellular processes) successfully in the past without negative impact on the anemones themselves as well as any symbiotic algae: Kanamycin (stock conc.: 50 mg/mL in ddH 2 O, working conc. in ASW: 100 µg/mL) - active against gram + and gram – bacteria Ampicillin (stock concentration: 100 mg/mL in ddH 2 O, working concentration: 200 µg/mL) - active against gram + and some gram – bacteria. If money is no issue, can be substituted by the more stable Carbenicillin. Streptomycin (stock conc.: 100 mg/mL in ddH 2 O, working conc.: 200 µg/mL) - active against gram – bacteria Tetracycline/Doxycycline (stock conc.: 10 mg/mL in 50% ethanol, working conc.: 10 µg/mL) - active against gram + and gram – bacteria; protect from light, store stock at −80°C OPTIONAL: Anidulafungin (stock concentration: 2 mg/mL in DMSO, working concentration: 2 µg/mL) - fungicide, expensive but Aiptasia-safe, use only if you have reason to suspect a fungal infection (or on VERY precious anemones) DO NOT USE the fungicide Nystatin! It immediately dissolves Aiptasia! Renew ASW with fresh antibiotic mix every two or three days for a week. It is no bad idea to have the last treatment over a weekend to make sure any remaining pathogens are killed or reduced to non-harmful levels.

Preparing brine shrimps for feeding: The best food source to use, with minimal upkeep and low costs, are brine shrimp ( Artemia spp.) nauplii. Dried brine shrimp cysts can be obtained through aquaculture vendors, we recently made the switch to cysts which have been treated so the shells are magnetic and empty shells as well as unhatched cysts can be removed after the hatching incubation with a (or better several) strong magnets which reduces the time and effort for cleaning enormously. We used to decapsulate the cysts with bleach before hatching (decapsulating one batch for one week of daily (Mon-Thu) hatching. But we found the elaborate and finicky decapsulation having no benefit over simply hatching more cysts directly and doing one or two more rounds of cleaning out the shells and unhatched cysts after hatching. The only advantage that decapsulation might present is a better nutrient content of the nauplii as they don't need to spend energy to break free from the cysts. With the magnetic cysts, decapsulation would be counter-productive. But for completeness, here is the legacy decapsulation protocol: HOW TO DECAPSULATE BRINE SHRIMP EGGS: Fill a 5-L glass beaker with 2400 mL dH 2 O, add a big stirring bar and place on a magnetic stir plate. Set the stirring to a medium level, so that a vortex forms on the top of the water but doesn't continue more than half-way down to the bottom. Both the glass beaker and the right stirring setting are important for quality of the eggs. Add an air tube to the beaker and adjust the air flow for a rolling bubble. Add ~70 g (corresponding to ~100-120 mL volume) of brine shrimp cysts. Aerate for 1-1.5 h. Check often to make sure airline hasn't fallen out and to swirl the eggs that build up along the bottom edges of the beaker. Add 800 mL of ChloroxPro Germicidal Bleach. Other bleach products might contain unwanted additives like chelators and surfactants. Continue to aerate for 3 to 3.5 min, swirling the airline the whole time. The solution should turn light brown and the eggs loose some of their color. Be careful not to exceed 4 min, proceed to the next step as soon as the solution turns frothy even if less than 3 min should have passed. Strain eggs through a shrimp net and rinse with DI water for 5 min or until you can't smell bleach. Squeeze the water out of the brine shrimp eggs. Fill eight 50-mL conical-bottom tubes with an equal volume of eggs (about 30-40 mL). Top each tube off with brine solution (saturated salt solution; ~55 g salt mix dissolved in 500 mL DI water and stored at 4°C. The eggs are now ready to be added to the hatching cone or stored at 4°C for later use within 1-2 weeks. Use 1-2 tubes for hatching in 6 L 20 g/L reduced-salinity salt water depending on feeding needs. HOW TO HATCH BRINE SHRIMP: Start hatching in the morning, so the nauplii can be fed the next morning to the anemones. Set up a large hatching cone (≥6 L usable volume) with a drain valve at the bottom connected to a tube to reach a sink, a strong light source above (or on the sides), an air line (a small flexible tube connected to an air pump and with an attached rigid plastic pipe long enough to reach the bottom of the cone) and optionally a small, fully enclosed and easy-to-clean heater and thermometer. Good aeration, high light levels (≥2500 lumen light source within 30 cm of cone) and a temperature between 27 and 30°C will result in a good brine shrimp yield. When using magnetic brine shrimp cysts, we found the best to have a rigid tube attached to the lid of the cone going reaching about 80-90% down into the cone with a sealed bottom into which a rod of strong N42 or N52 neodymium magnets can be inserted. Mix 4.5 L ASW with 1.5 L DI water in the hatching cone to make salt water with a salinity of ~25 g/L. Add brine shrimp cysts (20 g corresponding to a ~45 mL volume) to the hatching cone. Turn on air and light (and the optional heater). The air flow should be adjusted so that the cysts don't collect at the bottom but no excessive splashing at the water surface occurs. Monitor and rinse sides with ASW over the day to remove build up of eggs at water level. Hatch for 18-24 h. After hatching, turn off air and allow non-hatched cysts to settle and empty shells to float to the top (that should take 15-30 min). For magnetic brine shrimp cysts, insert the magnet rod into the tube before turning off the air so that unhatched cysts and empty shells are collected on the tube. Without magnets, unhatched cysts with a greyish-brown color will collect at the very bottom of the cone, bright orange nauplii will partly settle on top of that layer with a portion swimming above, and the greyish-brown empty shells will float to the top. Fill a small cone with 1 L ASW. Open the drain valve and collect nauplii in a large brine shrimp net in the sink. Try to avoid to collect the unhatched cysts from the very bottom and the empty shells from the top if not using magnets. Once all brine shrimp nauplii are collected, rinse brine shrimp with DI water (~20 s), squeeze out water, and transfer the nauplii into the small cone by inverting the net into the ASW and rinsing it with ASW over the cone. Add an air line to the small cone and aerate the water without splashing at the top. The nauplii can be maintained that way for the rest of the day and portions transferred into 500-mL squirt bottles for feeding anemones. OPTIONAL: Before feeding, remove air line for 10-15 min to have any unhatched cysts collect at the very bottom and most of orange nauplii settling on top and any remaining empty shells floating on top. Carefully decant the empty shells, then transfer the needed amount of the more concentrated nauplii into a 500-mL squirt bottle for feeding, top off the ASW in the cone to 1 L and re-insert the air line. For magnetic cysts, a smaller magnet on the outside of the cone can be used to capture any unhatched cysts or empty shells prior to feeding. Rinse down the sides of the large cone and any tubing and heaters/thermal probes with DI water until most cysts/debris are removed, scrub sides down with the sponge-stick, rinse again thoroughly and close valve. If used, remove the magnet rod before the cleaning and rinse-off the material collected on the protective tube. IF HATCHING A NEW BATCH OF NAUPLII FOR THE NEXT DAY: Start the hatching protocol over again. At the end of the day, empty the small brine shrimp cone, clean it with detergent (detach cap for cleaning), rinse with DI water and proceed with the instructions for bleaching containers (step 7 ff. in this protocol) . FOR PAUSES IN HATCHING ( e.g. , over the weekend): Fill the large cone with ~10 L DI water. Add about 100 mL of ChloroxPro Germicidal Bleach to the water and put a “Bleach” sign on the cone. Scrub down the sides thoroughly with the sponge stick. Rinse the sponge stick with DI water and leave in the cone. Add everything, which was in the cone during hatching back in, turn on the air line. Let sit for about an hour, then drain. Once emptied, close valve and add ~12 L DI water to the cone. Scrub down the sides with the sponge stick again, rinse sponge thoroughly with DI water and dry/store at a clean place. Drain the cone, quickly rinse the sides with DI water, close the valve, put the collecting end of the attached tubing into a clean 75-mL glass culture tube, and leave until next hatching. Detach the rigid tubes from the air lines, clean with a pipe brush the bottom ends where algae and/or brine shrimp debris might have accumulated and then treat as described as for bleaching containers (step 7 ff. in this protocol) .
Temperature: Stock tanks are generally kept at 27°C. Spawning tanks are kept at 29°C.

Light Levels: Symbiotic Aiptasia: 25±5 µmol photons/mm 2 /s measured at anemone level at multiple points on the shelf Aposymbiotic Aiptasia: 0 µmol photons/mm 2 /s (kept in black containers; can be brought out in the light for feeding and cleaning)
Light regimen: Anemones are kept at a "12 L:12 D" light regimen: 12 h light (as described above). 12 h darkness Time changes daylight saving time

Monitoring incubator conditions: We use HOBO loggers from Onset in a 1/9 tank with 200 mL ASW in every shelf of every incubator to monitor temperature and light levels at 1-h intervals and check the recorded data monthly.
Use disposable spatulas (the same as for tank cleaning) to gently dislodge the anemones and then use a large-orifice polyethylene transfer pipette to move them into a new container.

There are two possibilities for propagating Aiptasia: 1. PREFERRED: Aiptasia can be propagated by simply collecting pedal lacerates from stock tank, transferring them into new tanks and letting them develop into full polyps (usually a few days, not longer than a week for transition). This works well for strains which produce a reasonable amount of pedal lacerates (all our lab strains - CC7, H2, PLF3/F003, PLF5, PLF8). 2. Aiptasia can also be cut into halves (quarters with some practice and large anemones) with a razor blade or scalpel on a vertical axis through their oral disc. It is important to use clean/sterile blades for cutting to not introduce any pathogens or transmit microorganisms/symbionts between different tanks. Transfer the cut animals into a container with fresh ASW. Let them rest for 24-48 h (until they attach to the surface again), then feed them daily. They should be fully regenerated after ~1 week. The newly grown tentacles will be smaller and need some more weeks to reach the size of the old ones. Use this method only if pedal laceration is no option and give the animals several weeks to fully regenerate and regrow. The procedure might have impacts on the biology of the animals in experiments.
Air-dry on a clean rack.
Let the containers soak in DI water for ≥12 h. As a rule of thump, let them soak in DI water at least as long as they have been in the bleach bath.
Rinse with DI water.

Let the containers soak in a 0.65% (v/v) ChloroxPro Germicidal Bleach bath for ≥12 h. Use only commercial-grade ChloroxPro Germicidal Bleach! Other bleach products might contain unwanted additives like chelators and surfactants. Do not add metal to the bleach bath. We use big plastic crates with ~46 L DI water and add 300 mL bleach. ChloroxPro Germicidal Bleach has an effective sodium hypochlorite content of 8.25% (higher than the ~6% of consumer-grade bleach) , which results in a final sodium hypochlorite concentration of ~0.05% in the bath.

Clean used containers thoroughly with a suitable detergent and a brush until no biofilm/dirt is visible, rinse with de-ionized water. DO NOT get detergent in contact with bleach! We use Citranox ® detergent which has a low pH and is effective against salt/calcium deposits. The low pH also supports removing any DNA from the containers. There is another non-foaming alternative, CitraJet ® , which can be used in commercial-grade dish washers. But for manual cleaning the containers, the foam helps to indicate whether enough DI-water rinses have been performed.

Changing temperature or light conditions: Whenever changes in the program of an incubator need to be made, make sure to check the HOBO loggers after one or two expected cycles in light-dark (and/or temperature) in the case of otherwise empty incubators. If changes need to be made to incubators containing anemones/algae, ensure to be present for the first light change to confirm its proper execution and check closely after the next change to intervene if mistakes in the programing were made .

Daylight-saving time: We did not experience any problems from adjusting the ±1 h to/from daylight-saving time in one step. Not for the stock anemones nor for the spawners (just be sure to also adjust the "full-moon" timing accordingly). Use the same precautions as explained in the previous step and consider changing only one incubator per day, so that any severe mistakes do not affect all incubators.

We replace the bleach bath and DI water bath and cycle the plastic crates weekly. This prevents microorganisms/biofilms growing in the DI water bath. Depending on how many items cycle through the baths, a bi-weekly regimen might be fine as well.
creator: Christian CR Renicke
